options: Non-relational SQL
Not Only SQL
No Structured Query Language
Network Object Storage Language
questions.explanation: NoSQL means 'Not Only SQL' - these databases don't rely solely on relational tables and SQL. They support multiple data models including document, key-value, column, and graph stores while still potentially supporting SQL-like query languages.

questions.q: What are the characteristics of the BASE model used in NoSQL systems?
options: Binary, Atomic, Serialized, Exclusive
Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent
Batch, Aggregate, Search, Execute
Buffer, Archive, Store, Encrypt
questions.explanation: BASE is the NoSQL alternative to ACID: systems are basically available even during failures, data may be temporarily inconsistent (soft state), and consistency is eventual (achieved over time). This enables high availability and scalability.

questions.q: What is a document database optimized for?
options: Numeric computations
Storing and querying semi-structured JSON/BSON documents with flexible schemas
Fixed table structures with normalized relations
Real-time analytics on billions of rows
questions.explanation: Document databases (MongoDB, CouchDB) store nested, semi-structured data as documents. They allow flexible schemas where documents in the same collection can have different structures, suited for rapid development and hierarchical data.

questions.q: What is sharding in NoSQL databases?
options: Compressing data to reduce storage size
Partitioning data across multiple servers/nodes based on a shard key
Creating backups of the database
Encrypting sensitive columns
questions.explanation: Sharding distributes data horizontally across multiple machines based on a shard key (e.g., user_id). Each shard holds a subset of the data, enabling massive scalability and parallel processing, though it adds operational complexity.

questions.q: What is a key-value store optimized for?
options: Complex relational queries across multiple tables
Fast lookups and storage of simple key-to-value pairs with minimal overhead
Storing unstructured text documents
Running ACID transactions across distributed systems
questions.explanation: Key-value stores (Redis, Memcached, DynamoDB) provide ultra-fast O(1) lookups by storing and retrieving values using a unique key. They're ideal for caching, sessions, and real-time data but lack query flexibility.

questions.q: What is a column-family database used for?
options: Storing employee and family relationships
Real-time analytics and time-series data across billions of rows and millions of columns
Document-based storage with flexible schemas
Graph-based queries between entities
questions.explanation: Column-family stores (HBase, Cassandra) organize data by column rather than row, excelling at compressing time-series data and analytical queries. They support massive horizontal scaling and high write throughput for time-series databases.

questions.q: What is a graph database optimized for?
options: Simple key-value lookups
Querying relationships and connections between entities
Storing fixed-schema tabular data
Full-text search across documents
questions.explanation: Graph databases (Neo4j, ArangoDB) model data as nodes and edges, optimizing queries that traverse relationships. They excel at social networks, recommendation systems, and any domain where relationships are as important as the data itself.

questions.q: When should you use NoSQL instead of SQL databases?
options: Always; SQL is outdated
Never; SQL is superior
When you have unstructured data, need massive scale, or prioritize availability over strict consistency
Only for mobile applications
questions.explanation: NoSQL suits scenarios with flexible schemas, horizontal scalability needs, high write throughput, or when availability matters more than immediate consistency. SQL excels at complex queries, transactions, and structured relational data.

questions.q: What does eventual consistency mean in NoSQL systems?
options: Data is never consistent in NoSQL systems
All reads immediately see the latest writes
Data becomes consistent across all nodes over time, but temporary inconsistencies may exist
Consistency is checked once per day
questions.explanation: Eventual consistency means after a write, replicas synchronize over time, but brief windows exist where different nodes serve stale data. This trades immediate consistency for availability and partition tolerance in distributed systems.

questions.q: What is the primary advantage of NoSQL over relational databases for web applications?
options: NoSQL is always faster than SQL
Horizontal scalability and handling variable schema structures with high availability
NoSQL doesn't require any database administration
NoSQL supports complex transaction logic better than SQL
questions.explanation: NoSQL enables horizontal scaling by distributing data across servers and handles schema flexibility better than rigid relational models, making it ideal for rapidly growing web apps with unpredictable data structures and high availability requirements.
